data.title: FuboTV: Hulu Live Merger Lifts Bargaining Power, But Losses Muddy The Picture
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Media and Entertainment companies are navigating rapid changes in consumer behavior and preferences. Opening a position in FuboTV (FUBO) exposes an investor by default to the risks and opportunities arising from these disruptions.

Last October, Disney (DIS) acquired a 70% interest in FuboTV. This isn't a vote of confidence in FuboTV's live sports business. It is a calculated strategic move by Disney to bundle its struggling Hulu Live TV business into a public company, separate from its more successful streaming business. It is a separation with strategic motivations. First, it aligns the interest with a competitor. FuboTV was the company behind the failed live sports joint venture between Hulu Live TV, Fox Corp. (FOX), and Warner Brothers Discovery (WBD), having successfully sued against the establishment of the JV on antitrust grounds. Secondly, it houses a struggling business line into a publicly traded entity, allowing for separate capital-raising moves. Finally, it makes it easier to divest the business in the future. For FuboTV, the merger means more bargaining power with sports leagues and media broadcasters.

The question now is, will the combined entity create value for investors? This is not certain. Fubo has historically struggled to achieve profitability. Hulu Live TV isn't Fubo's knight in shining armor either. The Disney subsidiary is itself unprofitable.

This “hold” rating balances Disney's support for the new entity, offset by the long-term value destruction of FuboTV and Hulu Live.

Past Performance

Looking at the financials of both Hulu Live TV and FuboTV separately, the prospects of the combined entity don't seem encouraging. The deal closed on October 29, 2025. Fourth quarter results, often released in February of each year, will be the first quarter showing the combined financials.

On a TTM basis for the period ended September 2025, Fubo generated $1.6 billion in sales, flat compared to the same period of the year prior. FuboTV derives most of its revenue from subscriptions, a recurring revenue source that contributes about 92% of total sales. The remaining balance is mostly attributed to advertising revenue.

Hulu Live sales in the same periods grew modestly by 4.7% - $4.4 billion in the TTM ended September 2025, compared to $4.2 billion for the TTM ended September 2024. Sales consist mainly of wholesale revenue paid by media companies (mainly Hulu Streaming) - Hulu Live sells licensing rights to Hulu Streaming, which it bundles in a subscription bundle where subscribers get access to Hulu Streaming and the Live TV programs under one subscription.

What Hulu Live TV and FuboTV have in common is a focus on live TV. Their expenses are primarily licensing fees and broadcasting rights to third parties such as sports leagues and sports media networks. Here lies the problem. With high licensing fees and the inability to control other operating expenditures, the two entities have been unprofitable for years.

Net income (loss) $000s TTM Sep. 2025 TTM Sept 2024 TTM Sept. 2023
Hulu Live $ (156,283) $ (159,053) $ (172,320)
FuboTV $120,664 $ (207,886) $(317,978)

Note: The Net loss/income in the table above is from continuing operations. The period ending September 2025 includes a $220 million gain from a legal settlement against Disney, Warner Brothers Discovery, and Fox related to Venu, a sports TV JV. The parties settled in 2025. Excluding this gain, FuboTV's net loss from continuing operations would stand at roughly -$100 million.

Outlook

The combined entity benefits from two trends. 1) The improving cost structure of the FuboTV segment. 2) Exposure to the growing Hulu platform user base

However, neither the magnitude of the former nor the pace of the latter is enough to change the thesis here. Although the media business benefits from economies of scale, it is highly capital-intensive and, equally important, competitive.

Hulu Live TV revenue grew by roughly 4.7% YoY on a TTM basis as of September 2025. Net loss barely improved, standing at a loss of $156.3 million, down from a $159 million loss in the TTM ended September 2024. In other words, there is no meaningful margin improvement in this period.

FuboTV's revenue was flat YoY on a TTM basis. However, we're seeing an improved cost structure. The company achieved gross margin breakeven in mid-2023 (on a TTM basis), and it's been up since then. This is also manifested in the declining net losses in the table above.

So, how would the first quarter of the combined entity look?

Revenue is relatively stable. FuboTV is a subscription-based service, and while Hulu Live is technically a media distributor, the end users are ultimately subscribers, mainly to Hulu Streaming and Disney+ bundles that include Hulu Streaming and Live TV. This means that the impact of seasonality is minimal. Hulu Live is exposed to the success of Hulu Streaming (and even Disney+, to the extent that Hulu Live is included in marketing bundles). However, looking at Hulu Live's revenue trends, it seems that this exposure is indirect, and growth is not one-to-one. Hulu Live sales rose by a mere 4.7% in the last fiscal year, which ended in September 2025. Rising competition is a real issue here.

Assuming a flat revenue trajectory of the combined entity, revenue should fall in the $1.5 billion range in Q4'25 (calendar quarter ended December 2025).

Earnings should be flat in Q4'25 on a YoY basis (the deal closed in October 2025, so QoQ means Q4'25 compared to pro forma Q3'25 of the combined entity). We should see some higher professional fees tied to the merger, offsetting the profitability progress of FuboTV. We should see a net loss from continued operations at around a -$90 million range in Q4'25, consistent with the $357 million annual adjusted net loss in the TTM ended September 2025 for the combined entity (adjusted for FuboTV's $220 million legal settlement gain).

Balance Sheet

Disney isn't contributing much to the merger other than Hulu Live operations, consisting mostly of its business contract/relation with Hulu Streaming. Other than this relationship, Hulu Live doesn't have many assets. At the same time, Hulu Live has negative Cash Flows from Operations "CFO" in the magnitude of $166 million in the TTM ended September 2025. With no cash of its own, Hulu Live will depend on the cash of FuboTV, which itself has a liquidity problem.

FuboTV sits on $402 million in Current Assets ($274 million of which is cash & equivalents). Still, its current liabilities stood at $579 million as of September 2025. Its adjusted CFO for the TTM ended September stood at roughly -$80 million (when adjusted for the one-time legal settlement). The combined burn of the new entity exceeds its financial resources. In all likelihood, the new entity will raise capital in the coming quarters, diluting shares and/or increasing leverage.

Final Thoughts and Risks

FuboTV shares are down more than 90% in the past five years. The company has struggled to balance its costs (purchase of broadcasting licensing rights) with a highly competitive market.

Hulu Live isn't faring much better. It recorded losses for each year for the past three years (Note: Fiscal year measured as the TTM ended September). It has negative operating cash flows and has contributed zero cash to the new entity.

The new FuboTV will be bigger, but its losses and cash burn deeper.

The risk to this "Hold" thesis is the improving fundamentals of FuboTV. Net losses have narrowed in recent quarters, while operating cash burn has also improved (even when excluding the settlement gains). With the merger, the combined entity also has more negotiating power with sports leagues and broadcasting wholesalers. This constitutes the main threat to this "Hold" rating.

data.title: Canada Goose: Poised To Benefit From A Retail Rebound
data.description:
The stock market has kicked off 2026 on a very choppy note, with investors freshly worrying about heightened valuations and the possibility for escalating geopolitical and macroeconomic tensions, especially with the latest European tariff threat. Underneath this backdrop, I continue to encourage investors to position for safety in mind, and one of the best ways we can do so is by over-indexing our portfolios toward "unloved" sectors.

The retail and apparel space, in my view, has been overly bashed over the past year on fears of slowing consumer demand and margin hits from tariffs. All of these risks are true, and yet, stocks have already priced in these headwinds. Meanwhile, a number of specific brands have continued to showcase strong consumer momentum that makes their share price weakness, in my view, uncalled for.

Canada Goose (GOOS), in my opinion, is especially overdue for a rally. The stock is up over the past 12-month timeframe but has traded very choppily since last summer, despite the company managing to maintain strong sell-through, particularly in its DTC channels. To me, this is a great rebound opportunity for 2026.

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Canada Goose in August, when the stock was trading near $14 per share. Since then, Canada Goose has continued to trade in a very choppy fashion, even as the company has outperformed many of its retail peers with strong results. The next major catalyst for Canada Goose will be its fiscal Q3 earnings release, which encompasses the critical holiday quarter. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on Canada Goose ahead of that milestone.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Canada Goose, here is a refresher on what I view to be the long-term bull case for this company:

  • Differentiated, unique brand. Canada Goose is one of the original makers of heavy down winter parkas, and though many companies now vie for the luxury space (such as Arc'Teryx), Canada Goose's double-digit DTC growth is a telling signal that the brand remains an enduring classic.
  • Expansion of product lines. Canada Goose is committed to extending its brand popularity into a fuller lineup of products, including apparel and footwear, that can help diversify its revenue base and capture more of its customers' wallet share.
  • High gross margin profile. Canada Goose's ~60% gross margin more resembles a tech company than an apparel maker. The company's maintenance of its high margin showcases strong execution in the wake of tariffs.
  • Strong balance sheet management. The company has also slimmed down its inventory holdings and is paring down its debt load, freeing up the company and giving it plenty of financial flexibility.

Buy here ahead of the Q3 earnings release on Feb. 5.

Recent trends showcase strength

Let's first kick off the discussion with a recap of what has gone right for Canada Goose recently. To me, one of the most important positive indicators is the fact that the company has achieved significant DTC channel growth, which is the most telling indicator of healthy demand.

As shown in the chart above, the company achieved 21% y/y growth in the DTC channel to C$126.6 million in the quarter ending in September, or just shy of half of overall sales. DTC growth roughly kept pace with 23% y/y growth in Q1, which is a less-meaningful quarter for Canada Goose (given few shoppers are buying winter gear during the summer months).

Furthermore, it's not just new store openings that are driving DTC store growth for Canada Goose (the company has been investing heavily of late to build out its store network in China, which, needless to say, is a strategic move designed to build brand market share in the world's largest luxury consumer market). Management noted that Canada Goose achieved 10% comp sales growth in DTC channels in Q2, which excludes the contribution of newly opened (and recently closed) stores. Furthermore, the company noted that comp sales have been positive for 10 straight quarters, and were individually positive in each country/market in which Canada Goose operates.

In today's weaker consumer economy, positive comps are far from a given. Canada Goose's 10% comps growth in the DTC channel showcases very healthy demand for the brand, especially when many luxury names are facing weaker sales.

Of course, the offset here is weaker wholesale revenue, declining 5% y/y in the most recent quarter. Shipment timing plays a factor in y/y comps here, so the company notes that across the longer arc of time in the first half of FY26, wholesale revenue was flat.

It makes sense here that Canada Goose retailers are less willing to take on inventory in the wake of weaker sales growth. That said, double-digit DTC comps growth is a clear signal that the brand remains popular in difficult times. Canada Goose's CEO Dani Reiss also noted on the Q2 earnings call that wholesale sell-through was healthy, and that positive comps continued through October:

Wholesale represented about half of our total revenue in Q2, which is typically our largest wholesale quarter. Revenue for the quarter was in line with our expectations, slightly down year-over-year and flat through the first half. We see strong positive leading indicators in the channel with stronger sell-through globally, improved inventory health and greater product diversity and excitement from our partners about our upcoming collections [...]

We are very happy to report that the second half is off to a strong start with positive DTC comps in October.

These are all signals that Canada Goose is positioning itself for wholesale restocking orders, potentially turning wholesale trends around in the second half of FY26 (channel inventory needs to be replaced, if sell-through outpaces shipments).

Canada Goose's own inventory is also declining, down 3% y/y to C$460.7 million in the most recent quarter (despite tariffs presumably increasing average cost per item, indicating that inventory units are down even sharper). All of these are healthy indicators that the company is moving product at a healthier clip than many peers amid the consumer downturn.

Q3 look-ahead, valuation, and key takeaways

With this recent context in mind, here are the items I'm watching for in Q3:

  • DTC trend maintenance. We think Canada Goose will rally if it can keep up >20% DTC sales growth through the critical holiday quarter, which is Canada Goose's biggest season. The recent retail industry ICR conference appeared to give a picture of healthy holiday shopping, which hopefully applies to Canada Goose as well.
  • Wholesale sell-through trends. So far, management has only anecdotally pointed to the fact that wholesale sell-through is healthy, as wholesale revenue is flat to declining. We're hoping that the sell-through trends continue as well, and the best way to tell that sell-through is outpacing shipments is if wholesale revenue turns positive in Q3/Q4, signaling restocking.
  • Investment plans for 2026. We're also hoping for more clarity on Canada Goose's investment plans for calendar 2026, particularly around new store openings. The company just built out a new flagship on the Champs-Elysees in Paris. We largely expect the company to slow down its pace of flagship store openings and focus on bumping existing comps as well as online sales, which may have a positive impact on cash flow and margins.

Despite its many strengths, we continue to find Canada Goose attractively priced at just 8x forward adjusted EBITDA:

Again, I think Canada Goose's upcoming Q2 earnings will show investors that actual fundamental trends at the company are healthier than many retail peers, and cast a light on the stock's very modest valuation multiples to position it for an upward re-rating. "Buy" here with confidence.

data.title: Henkel: The Upside For The Long-Term Is Excellent In 2026
data.description:
Henkel (HENKY) "blossomed" last time during 2021 after COVID-19. By that description, I mean that that was the last time the company traded above its historical premium in any meaningful way. For almost a year at the time of writing this article, Henkel has been trading at a significant discount to even conservative estimates for the company's profitability. I have been taking advantage of this undervaluation by investing in the company, and I intend to continue doing this either until the company's valuation is above where it's attractive, or until my position is at such a size that it's no longer acceptable with my targets and allocations to continue investing in it.

This update for Henkel marks my first 2026 article for the company. Some analysts would call Henkel a longer-term disappointment. I would not hold with that view - there have been clear times when it's been advisable to rotate the position and take what profits you may. Even if Henkel has perhaps not performed as well as some might expect, there are reasons for this - these are what I will be reminding you of in this article, and where I will show you my positive overall stance (as well as what it's based on).

You might consider Henkel mostly a detergent/laundry and glue company, and while technically this is correct, it certainly misses important nuances that I believe to be crucial to an upside stance here.

I've always been focused more on the company's industrial and commercial upside than its personal care/consumer upside. There's a reason for that - I believe this side of the company's business to be undervalued. In this article, I will show you exactly why that is.

Henkel's upside in 2026

You can find my last article on Henkel here - basically saying that there is upside despite the underperformance. In this article, I mean to crystallize some of the arguments made therein, and perhaps bring a few new ones to the table as well.

3Q25 is the latest set of results we have, with the full-year coming early this year. The 3Q results confirmed further upside for the company with both organic sales growth and operational improvements. While I focus a lot on the adhesive part of the company (more "sticky", if you pardon the pun), it's close to a 50/50 split between Adhesive and Consumer brands if we look at sales. However, one of the segments is growing a lot better than the other.

Henkel is also a company where I have no issue with ongoing or future share buybacks - this has to do with the valuation of the company, which makes those €700M of buybacks attractive, and which makes it interesting that the company is still trading so cheaply despite these ongoing buybacks.

You can't be positive on things like AI, sustainability, ESG, connectivity, and general digitization trends without in turn being positive on Henkel. The reason I say this is because Henkel, through its segment in Adhesive, is an integral part of this push into new and rising megatrends.

Henkel's products and existing market segments/exposure make the company crucial for Semiconductors and their packaging, many consumer/commercial digital devices, Industrials as well as automotive.

What Henkel does for these segments and these trends is provide solutions for things like thermal management, protection, and bonding. This is the core reason why I believe we'll see high single-digit or low double-digit growth for the company from 2026-2030 at the very least, and potentially beyond. This can be exemplified through something seemingly simple like a Camera module, which requires the company solutions to even work properly (not that there aren't other providers, it's just that Henkel is one of the biggest).

How exactly does Henkel help semi manufacturers and users of these modern technologies do "their thing"? Aside from adhesives as seen above, the company is a manufacturer of so-called TIMs, or Thermal Interface Materials, which conduct heat away from the device - including things like sintering materials, pastes, and the like. This is only a complement to the already-known so-called Underfilling technologies, which protect customer products from damage from physical impact and cycling (thermal). The brand LOCTITE, which you've no doubt heard about, is one of the primary products here. But aside from that, it's also general adhesives for die-attaching and general semiconductor packaging. By "packaging", I do not mean in this context the production or work with physical packages, I actually mean technologies for things like the surface mounting of chip packages, which involves connection to circuit balls - such as Ball Grid Array (BGA) technologies and the like.

But Henkel also works with other high-tech processes, such as sintering (mentioned above) solutions, which bind powder into a solid by heating them to below-melting-point temperatures, causing a contact-point bonding.

Again, this is by far my favorite part of the company. It's not that the laundry and home care segment is uninteresting - the company has market leadership here as well.

Whenever I travel to one of my homes/work locations, I'm often surprised at how ubiquitous the company's brands are, when I can even find them in a small local store in the countryside in East Africa, or in a port in the Middle East. But that is the global world for you.

However, I don't think it's unfair to say that the company's home brands is subject to a lot more volatility and competition than the industrial and commercial markets. And while I am thrilled about the company's focus on new areas, I'm less thrilled with some of the ventures it currently does, such as its latest "Gen AI ad" in Germany. My problem with ads like these, or even content, is that it lacks the authenticity and creative quality of human-created ads, and can lead to consumer distrust of the brand. (But this is, admittedly, a very minor point in what was otherwise a very solid set of results.)

My worries for home care/consumer seem to be unfounded, given that the company recorded significant market share gains (over 180 bps) in key brand Perwoll.

What exactly is the problem here, if we look at the sort of stagnant underperformance that we've seen for many months now?

From what I can find, the primary antagonist to the company's future is fears about the changing dynamics in legacy consumer products, like Beauty and home care, as well as the company's historical slowness in addressing some of its fundamental portfolio issues. It's not out of the question at this point or in the future that more changes in the portfolio may be required. But I don't see why this risk, or any risk in the commercial/industrial sector, should give rise to this valuation, when Henkel is expecting positive organic sales growth of 1-2%, or a solid 15%+ potential EBIT margin. The macro certainly remains volatile - but given the company's exposures, this should be of only small impact for the business. This is also especially true with significant, above-market growth in emerging markets- IMEA grew by over 10% as of 3Q25.

The company has also seen adhesive turnaround in terms of pricing, and I expect the coming period to continue this acceleration (and this includes 2026 as well).

Let's see about the valuation for the company.

Valuation for Henkel in 2026

The reason I am updating here in January is because I believe that the company represents an even smaller subsection of attractive businesses to invest in here than before.

Henkel is a company, in my view, with a moat. If you don't believe the company has a moat, you're saying the adhesive technology segment and its application don't have compelling moatworthy upside. You're also saying that despite Henkel being a market leader, more than 3x the size of the next one, it's not worth a moat or a premium for what amounts to over 60% of its adjusted operating profit.

I believe such a stance would be foolish. Also remember, the closest peers for the business include companies like Unilever (UL) or Procter & Gamble (PG), which come with entirely different exposure profiles. Henkel also has the second-most attractive yield in its peer group, close to 3%.

Even if we only use mid-cycle margin assumptions and normalized 1-3% organic growth rates, a company with this market exposure and portfolio should be significantly higher than it currently is. That is why I give it a fair value in the triple digits, and am raising it to €105/share as of early 2026. €105/share represents the 5-year average of 16.5x P/E with a 2-3% organic growth rate, and 4-5% AEPS growth until 2026E. That means an upside of 16% annualized if you were to invest here.

I ascribe Henkel a higher growth rate because it's a market leader, and the player with the most diverse portfolio for its solutions. I don't believe the last few years of meager margins are indicative of the future margins. I believe this supports not only an FV increase, but a PT increase from €75/share to €77/share to account for the higher upside.

The biggest risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The main risk for Henkel is related to its consumer segments, which I believe are sub-par in terms of market-beating upside even after the divestments - that's why I focus so much on the industrial side of things. While the company has leadership in key segments, I don't see how the company would be able to drive profit with just this (confirmed by more than 60% of operating profit coming from adhesives, but only 50% of revenues). So, in forecasting Henkel, we need to discount that, and I have - if the company was a pure-play industrial, the share price would be put at 10-20% higher in terms of targets.

Another central risk to an investment here is underperformance due to anemic growth. Even if I believe this to be about to change, I would be wrong in not at least mentioning this here. Current estimates are at 4-5%, but Henkel has a history (25%) of unfortunately missing estimates. I believe at 15-17x P/E as opposed to 17.5-19x P/E, I'm discounting properly for this, but in the event of underperformance, it could prove inadequate.

Furthermore, as the "icing" on the cake here in terms of risk, look at COGS exposure. Much of the company's costs are made efficient and improved - but the company can't "efficiency away" input costs and commodity risks. Raw material price cycles are another central risk to the business.

That's the primary risk as of 2026 - but one I believe is baked into the current share price and my fair value target.

Thesis

Henkel is one of the more qualitative players in this entire segment. Peers in adhesive peers offer alternatives, but the margins speak for themselves, as does the company's market share, 3x the share of the closest competitor.

Henkel seems to have, as of 3Q25 and going into 2026, righted some of its underperforming segments and operations. With its refreshed focus including a higher upside from Adhesives due to things like AI pushes, I believe the company is in a prime position for an undervalued investment. This is especially in a market like the one we're seeing, which I believe to be significantly inflated.

I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here in early 2026. My price target is €77/share, updated for 2026, with a fair value target of roughly €105/share. For the HEN3 ADR, which is HENOY, the PT comes to $22.5.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €77/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tharimmune, Inc. And The Canton Network: A Privacy Focused, Asymmetric Bet On Tokenization
data.description:
Introduction

Tharimmune, Inc. (THAR) is a pre-revenue, biotech research and development firm. In their most recently published financials, this thinly traded, microcap stock with no long-term debt showed $10.3 million in annual expenses and $7.6 million in cash on hand, giving them roughly a 9-month runway. However, on November 6th, 2025, the company radically changed, announcing a $545 million issuance of common stock or pre-funded warrants to fund the establishment of a Canton Coin (CC-USD) digital asset treasury. This shift in strategy turned Tharimmune into the first and only publicly traded digital asset treasury company for the Canton Coin. The move was funded by well-established players in the financial industry, and we will get into who that includes. But first, we need to focus on the underlying asset itself: Canton Coin.

The Canton Network and its Coin

The Canton Network was created by a company called Digital Asset and is a smart contract enabled, public but permissioned ledger. Founded in 2014, the funding for this company over the years has come from the likes of The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), Citadel Securities, The Depository Trust and Clearing Corporation (DTCC), Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK), S&P Global Inc. (SPGI), and Nasdaq, Inc. (NDAC). It was built to meet their needs and preferences.

This has ruffled the feathers of idealistic crypto enthusiasts who champion open, decentralized, permissionless blockchain technology. Digital Asset has taken the opposite approach, prioritizing privacy, permissions, and a more centralized governance model. I’m not here to judge one way or the other. I’m just here to offer my best estimation on how to profit.

The main thesis behind the network can be found in the Canton Network’s whitepaper. In it, the company makes the case that there are two problems limiting institutional adoption of existing smart contract networks. First the paper states that current smart contract networks require users to adopt fully transparent privacy models. This can be a problem for traditional financial institutions for obvious reasons. The second issue that the whitepaper addresses is that users compete for transaction throughput. In a world that thrives on predictability, this poses a problem for organizations looking for predictable pricing and consistent timeliness of transactions. So, the Canton network was created to address these limitations allowing each user to define their privacy parameters and scaling requirements while utilizing a public but permissioned network. To achieve privacy, they effectively restrict the data that each party, depending on their role, can view to where no one in the network can see the full details of a transaction. To address speed, Canton uses subnets (similar concept to an L2 but they are interoperable and still contained within Canton’s single network) allowing transactions to be ordered locally.

Canton Network incentivizes its participants with the Canton Coin, a utility token which began trading publicly in November of 2025. Unlike traditional cryptocurrencies which only incentivize validators with rewards, Canton actually incentivizes users that build applications on the network as well. The Canton Coin Whitepaper breaks participants down into 4 categories:

  1. Users: Basically, anyone that holds the coin
  2. Validator: Node that provides network access to users and validate transactions on the network (infrastructure provider)
  3. Super Validator (SV): Node that validates and synchronizes transactions (infrastructure provider)
  4. Application Provider: A validator that also conducts transactions of their own on the network and submits a portion of those transactions to the synchronizer

Validators, super validators, and application providers are all rewarded with the ability to mint canton coins for building and processing. The pace of that minting and the allocation of the coins is shown below:

As you can see, in the initial years the bulk of newly minted coins will go to the validators and super validators. As the infrastructure providers of the network, this incentivizes their build out. But over time, that prioritization shifts and by year ten, 75% of all new coins will be minted by those who build and operate applications on the network. This is to incentivize continued growth of the network. After year 10 there will be 100 billion coins, and the supply of newly minted coins is capped at 2.5 billion annually.

Fees are priced in US Dollars to provide stable, predictable pricing. But with each transaction a corresponding number of Canton Coins based on the current conversion rate are burned as part of Canton’s burn-mint equilibrium mechanism. The way this works according to the coins white paper is as follows: users and application providers pay transaction or resource usage fees (denominated in USD) not by directly paying but by burning the corresponding number of Canton Coins. So, as the price of Canton Coin drops, more are burned with each transaction and as it rises, less are burned with each transaction. Users and application providers have the option of acquiring and burning the coins themselves or they can pay a third party to do it for them so that they are not required to actually hold Canton Coin on their balance sheet. The validators, SVs, and application providers then get to mint the appropriate amount of Canton Coin to maintain the minting curve as their reward for services provided. The aspiration of this model is to keep the supply of Canton Coin stable while avoiding supply shortages or speculation. Digital Asset’s goal was to create a system where the price of the coin reflects the utility provided by the network, adequately rewarding those who build and maintain it.

The final characteristic of the system that is worth mentioning is how to become a validator. With Bitcoin or Ethereum for example, anyone can join the network and validate transactions. With Canton, those who want to validate transactions must be sponsored by an existing SV and apply for permission from a validators committee. This mechanism helps to ensure that the members of the network making governance decisions are aligned.

Current State of the Network

With the latest data showing $1.72 million in daily fees, this translates to an annualized number of $631.45 million. The current network value or market cap is $5.61 billion placing the network value at 8.9x the estimated annualized fees. Canton's average transaction fee is not currently published but working backwards from the data linked above, I estimate the transaction fee averages out to roughly $2.31 ($1.72 million / 743 thousand transactions = $2.31). For a comparative example, Ethereum currently has an average transaction price of .331 cents and is doing about 2.9 million transactions per day. Annualized this translates to roughly $960 million dollars in fee revenue compared to a market cap of $398 billion. This places the network value at roughly 414x the estimated annual fees.

The list of companies acting as super validators on the network include Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL), Chainlink, Tradeweb Markets Inc. (TW), Broadridge, and Nasdaq. When it comes to application provider highlights, DTCC announced Canton will be the first blockchain they use to begin tokenizing assets and transacting. The DTCC processed over $3.7 quadrillion worth of transactions in 2024 and provides custody services for $99 trillion worth of assets. JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM) recently announced they will be offering their JPM Coin on the Canton Network. Despite the network just going public in November, there is already robust usage from the world’s most recognizable financial institutions and based on these recent announcements future usage growth looks probable.

In Walks Tharimmune

Many people criticize DATs and say that it is better to just buy the underlying asset. But Canton is different and that argument, while entertainable with other cryptocurrencies, has little merit when it comes to Canton Coin. The entities minting fresh Canton Coin are all large institutions that were sponsored, applied and accepted as validators. With the right set up, I can stake my own Ethereum or mine for Bitcoin but I cannot just mint new Canton Coin without permission, giving Canton Coin DATs that become Super Validators a distinct advantage over individuals that just own the underlying asset. This structure makes Tharimmune instantly relevant.

The details on their treasury strategy are still limited. So, if we are going to evaluate the stock, we need to start making some assumptions. The announcement stated that out of the $545 million Tharimmune would pay transaction fees and set some aside to fund operations. $45 million seems high but let’s use that number because it is nice and round and say that they will use $500 million to acquire Canton Coin. At current network value, this would give Tharimmune just roughly 9% of the current supply of Canton Coin. Whether they will be more or less aggressive is not clear yet, but it is within the realm of possibility.

One way Tharimmune could generate revenue with their supply is by being a service provider for those using the network. Remember, to transact on the network, parties must burn Canton Coin. However, some of these companies will likely not want to hold Canton Coin on their balance sheet. The Canton Coin white paper states “Participant node operators that want to connect to the Global Synchronizer can use Canton Coin to prepay their traffic directly, or they can arrange with a third party to set up a traffic balance (like a cell phone data plan paid in fiat currency) on their behalf.” Tharimmune could be that third party, providing the service of holding and burning Canton Coin. This could create a subscription like revenue model with consistent cashflow. They have not announced this as part of their strategy but by understanding how the network operates it becomes a clear opportunity to productively use their supply of Canton Coin. The next source of revenue for Tharimmune comes from acting as a Super Validator, allowing them to mint new Canton Coins. This would be similar to a Bitcoin DAT that mines for Bitcoin or an Ethereum DAT that stakes their Ethereum. Becoming a Super Validator is part of Tharimmune's announced strategy. Additionally, Tharimmune has announced plans to invest in companies that will be building on the Canton Network. This sounds similar to BMNR’s (an ETH DAT) moonshots strategy which I detailed in a previous article.

Tharimmune’s pivot to become a Canton Coin DAT was funded by some fairly recognizable and established names. The following comes from the company's November 3rd, 2025 8k: "DRW and Liberty City Ventures acted as the lead investors, with participation by many prominent investors including ARK Invest, Bitwave, Broadridge, Clear Street, Copper, Digital Asset, Feynman Point Asset Management, Five North, Kaiko, Kenetic, Kraken, LendOS, Lukka, Nima Capital, MPCH, Obsidian Software, Polychain Capital, Proof Group, SBI Group, The Tie, Tradeweb Markets, and 7RIDGE." While the total amount contributed by each investor has not been publicly disclosed, I'll highlight a few points. The Canton Foundation and Digital Asset provided some of the funding. In my opinion, the fact that both are backing Tharimmune signals two significant pieces of information. First, it shows that Digital Asset and the Canton Foundation are putting their thumb on the scale, actively helping to shape the market in the Canton Networks favor. Second, it means Tharimmune is indirectly funded by those funding Digital Asset- companies like Nasdaq, Bank of New York Mellon, and S&P Global. Additionally, DRW, Tradeweb, and Broadridge are also worth highlighting as investors in THAR. DRW, one of the two lead investors, processes roughly 1 million transactions per day while Broadridge and Tradeweb represent a combined daily trading volume of $12.8 trillion. If Tharimmune does decide to offer burning as a service, companies like Broadridge, Tradeweb, DRW, Nasdaq, Bank of New York Mellon, and S&P Global might just turn into their customer base.

To facilitate this pivot, Tharimmune brought in a new President, CEO, and CFO all of which come with decades of experience in the financial industry. I’ll highlight Mark Wedland, the new CEO as of November. Previously, Mark served as a treasurer at Citadel and at DRW before taking on the COO role for DRW. Citadel, a well-known market maker and liquidity provider, was one of the early investors in Digital Asset and the Canton Network. When it comes to DRW, one of their primary functions is to serve as a liquidity provider in various markets, including crypto assets. This background makes Mark extremely well suited to lead Tharimmune as a Canton Coin treasury.

THAR's most recent S-3 stated that this shift in strategy will not materially change their day-to-day operations in the biotech business. Currently, their pipeline includes two products and oncology research efforts:

1. TH104 is a buccal film technology built to protect military and first responders involved in chemical incident response. The company is clear to file a new drug application with the FDA, as no further testing is required for this indication.

2.TH023, an oral antibody used to treat autoimmune conditions, is currently being prepared for phase 1 trials

3. Gravitas Life Sciences: THAR's oncology segment is currently researching two potential therapies. It is not clear if or when these treatments will enter clinical trials.

While it is important to monitor Tharimmune's pipeline, Canton Coin is the primary driver of Tharimmune's stock performance going forward. Tharimmune's pivot is a massive bet on the future of the Canton Network. The biotech side of the business will at best be a source of positive upside surprise and at worst a temporary drag on growth.

Risks

Right now, one of the biggest risks is opacity. This strategy is in its early days and as a small company, Tharimmine has less stringent reporting requirements. This risk will diminish as time passes and more information is disclosed but for now, we are lacking information. On the financial front, we don't know how much Canton Coin they own. On the strategy front, we've only received broad strokes with little detail. Ultimately, if they do not capitalize on the ability to become a service provider for the burning of Canton Coin, I might consider selling the stock.

The stock is also currently non-compliant with Nasdaq and at risk of delisting due to the composition of their board. Tharimmune filed an 8-k stating they anticipate this issue will be remedied at their upcoming shareholders meeting on January 30th. It appears to be a simple enough fix, but still an issue worth monitoring.

The price of Canton Coin also poses a risk. Because it just became available to trade in November, we have no idea what the price action will be like. In the long term, Canton Coin may be less volatile compared to other crypto currencies because of the burn mint structure. But if the network is not adopted the coin’s value will be inflated away to effectively $0. It is not clear how this burn mint structure will impact the value of the coin even if there is massive adoption. My best guess is that the 2.5 billion coins that are minted per year after year ten will need to be valued high enough to at least cover the costs incurred by validators and super validators. But it is possible that the Canton Network could just become basically free infrastructure, used by major institutions where the operation of the network is done at a loss and the efficiency gains are all captured by the individual businesses that use the network effectively.

Conclusion

Tharimmune raised $545 million at $3.075 per share. When the announcement was made the stock rallied to around $4 but has since declined back to $3.01, offering retail investors the ability to purchase shares at the same price where large institutional investors recently valued the shares.

The current lack of details surrounding long term strategy and financial position is providing this opportunity. But I believe to understand the opportunity ahead, we just need to be paying attention. The biggest financial players in the world are telling us they are going to use the Canton Network. They’ve funded it, slowly built it and tested it over the past decade. Now it is going public and the financial giants of the world are moving on to it. Some of these companies may not want to hold Canton Coin on their balance sheets because it is an asset with very little history. As a result, they will want someone to do that for them. I think this is where Tharimmune will play a pivotal role, as they could offer burning as a service to the financial community on the Canton Network enabling Tharimmune to effectively sell Canton Coin at a premium while continuously minting new coins as a Super Validator. This is obviously highly speculative. Do not invest in Tharimmune what you cannot afford to lose. But right now, retail investors have an opportunity to invest at the same price as those who funded the shift and who possess a deep understanding of this project. Additionally, Canton Coin is in its infancy. Unlike more established cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin or Ethereum, there are no ETFs for it, there are no other DATs, banks are not accepting it as collateral yet, and no governments hold it on their balance sheet. Canton Coin has all of that potential still in front of it. This is a highly asymmetric bet that will play out over the next decade. I’ve bought Tharimmune. I know my downside. But the potential upside is hard to fathom. Either way, I’m excited to see where this goes.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed-Income Monitor: January 2026
data.description:
By Samantha Parsons

Determining how to allocate to fixed income requires careful consideration. Our monthly Fixed-Income Monitor consolidates the views of our investment team to help identify opportunities and risks across the asset class.

Key takeaways for January 2026

  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered a 25-basis-point (bp) cut in December, lowering the target range to 3.50%-3.75%. This marked the third reduction of 2025, totaling 75 bps for the year. Fed Chair Jerome Powell communicated caution on future cuts amid inflation concerns.
  • U.S. Treasury yields showed muted reactions, as markets had already priced in the decision. Longer term rates remained elevated given uncertainty about future cuts.
  • The (delayed) November unemployment rate rose to 4.6%, the highest in four years, alongside a modest gain of 64,000 payrolls, further suggesting a gradual cooling in the labor market.
  • After early-month volatility, corporate credit posted solid gains. Investment-grade bonds outperformed high yield, supported by lower Treasury rates and strong fundamentals. Spreads tightened across sectors, signaling investor confidence despite macro uncertainty, but security selection remains key.

Yields and credit spread tracker

The proprietary fixed-income tracker compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income, which can help investors evaluate how they are being compensated for taking on risk.1

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments as of December 15, 2025. Each bar represents the range for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Yield is represented by yield to worst, which is the minimum return received on a callable bond, apart from the yield if the issuer were to default. For municipal bonds, yield is represented by taxable equivalent yield, which is based on the top federal bracket (37%) and net investment income tax (3.8%). Other taxes are possible. Yield percentile and spread percentile are represented by the range of daily yields and daily spreads, respectively, and both are for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

Fixed income sector positioning

Our fixed income team analyzes fundamentals, valuations and technicals to assess relative value in bond markets. This approach can help investors position for risks and opportunities across sectors within the asset class.

The bottom line

Fixed income plays a crucial role in a well-diversified portfolio. Our investment team uses bottom-up company and sector analysis with top-down macroeconomic analysis to build fixed-income portfolios.

Fixed income portfolio allocations should reflect personal financial goals and risk tolerance; a financial advisor can help tailor a strategy to fit an individual investor’s needs.

Disclosures

1.Interpreting yields and spreads: Spread refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries), and indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk. If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less. There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. Income from tax-exempt municipal bonds or municipal bond funds may be subject to state and local taxes, and a portion of income may be subject to the federal and/or state alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Federal and state income tax rules will apply to any capital gains.

© 2016-2026 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Use of products, materials and services available through Columbia Threadneedle Investments may be subject to approval by your home office.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and are managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC. Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC serves as the investment manager to the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. (Member FINRA) is the distributor for Columbia Threadneedle Investments ETFs. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., (Member FINRA) is a marketing agent for the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. is not affiliated with Columbia Threadneedle Investments.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/23/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Energy Stocks Steady Amid Macro Chaos, With A Sunday Night Earnings Surprise Ahead
data.description:
It was a sea of red to kick off the holiday-shortened trading week yesterday. President Trump’s ambition to annex part or all of Greenland drew backlash from European leaders. A new set of tariffs also roiled markets, precisely one year after the 47th POTUS took office.

In terms of market action, the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) spiked above 20, while the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) suffered its worst session since last August. But it wasn’t solely a U.S.-Europe macro story.

The Land of the Rising Sun.... And Falling Fixed Income Market

An outright bond market implosion occurred in Japan. The island nation’s 40-year yield skyrocketed to a record above 4.20%, and its benchmark 30-year rate jumped 27 basis points to a fresh all-time high of 3.88%. Worries over tax cuts and their impact on Japan’s fiscal situation continued a macro trend ongoing for the past year-plus.

All eyes will be on the upcoming Bank of Japan monetary policy meeting, ahead of the U.S. Federal Reserve’s January 28 interest-rate decision.

Energy Atop the Early-Year Sector Leaderboard, High Macro Volatility

For investors, 10 of the 11 S&P 500 sectors traded in the red yesterday morning. The lone bright spot? Energy.

The oil & gas space often offers diversification benefits to global portfolio managers when macro jitters take center stage. Shares of Exxon Mobil (XOM), the world’s most valuable publicly traded energy company, tagged a record high above $131 for the first time. It has been remarkable how resilient large energy firms have been, given depressed oil prices.

Indeed, both WTI and Brent crude have felt the brunt of a global oil glut. President Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” mindset and OPEC's unsuccessful attempts to stabilize the supply-demand balance have kept oil bears in charge.

Though prices spiked earlier this month when the U.S. captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro (it feels like ages ago at this point), prices quickly fell back down. American households probably aren’t complaining, as AAA reports that the average cost of a gallon of regular unleaded is near five-year lows at $2.82.

Don’t Forget about Natural Gas: Old Man Winter Tightens His Grip

Stable oil prices contrast what’s happening in the natural gas markets. The February 2026 contract of U.S. Henry Hub gas jumped nearly 30% on Tuesday. Forecasts for bone-chilling cold over the final week of January spooked markets, and this weekend’s wintry blast could rival what transpired in Texas four years ago, just as power grids grapple with rising prices and pressure from policymakers. Impacts are likely from the Lone Star State to the Mid-Atlantic.

Earnings Events Add Drama and Investor Interest, BKR Stands Out

So, there’s no shortage of drama at home and abroad, as well as across oil and natural gas markets, for energy executives to weigh. Near-term risks and long-term opportunities are aplenty at the moment.

That will make the upcoming round of energy sector Q4 reports all the more interesting. Additionally, our team uncovered an outlier company that will report off schedule this coming Sunday night.

What is that firm? Baker Hughes (BKR). Shares of the $41 billion market-cap oil and gas equipment and services company hover near record highs ahead of its unusual weekend earnings event. There hasn’t been significant news lately - we have to go back to last summer, when BKR acquired Chart Industries (GTLS) in a $13.6 billion M&A move.

Market pricing also suggests that the January 25 Q4 report might not include many fireworks. According to data from Option Research & Technology Services, the stock is expected to move just 4.8% (near its long-term average earnings-related straddle of 4.9%). Shares dipped 3.3% following the October Q3 report.1

We also dug into the corporate event calendar. BKR’s quarterly conference call takes place Monday morning next week, but a quiet stretch then ensues - no major volatility catalysts until its April Q1 report. Nevertheless, be on guard for potential one-time items to be included in Sunday’s release.

Listen Up!

Beyond Baker Hughes, and after Halliburton (HAL) reported on Wednesday morning, SLB Corp. (SLB), formerly known as Schlumberger, serves up numbers Friday before the bell.

The heavy hitters - XOM and Chevron (CVX) - report next Friday, January 30. Investors will listen intently to what CEOs Darren Woods and Mike Wirth have to say about the volatile state of the global energy market, particularly regarding Venezuela.

Opportunities in South America?

Much has been made of the country’s oil reserves, which are likely materially lower than what the Maduro and former Chávez regimes have claimed. Moreover, while resource-rich, Venezuelan oil is notoriously sour and dense, more like tar than domestic light sweet crude.

In short, establishing a further U.S. presence there will take years, so don’t expect any concrete developments to be unveiled next Friday morning. Still, Chevron’s existing Venezuela assets could be optimized to extract more “black gold” in the quarters ahead.

Bulls Gaining Steam

As for price action, XOM and CVX are up almost 9% so far in 2026, outperforming the S&P 500 by more than eight percentage points. Momentum is solid, and income investors may be drawn to those stocks’ dividend yields (3.2% for XOM and 4.1% for CVX).

Elsewhere, the energy conference tour heats up in March. Three notable events take place toward the end of Q1:

  • March 2: Jefferies Flagship Power, Utilities, Clean Energy & Energy Conference

  • March 16: Piper Sandler 26th Annual Energy Conference

  • March 23: CERA Week

The Bottom Line

The Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) leads among the 11 S&P 500 sector funds. Up more than 6% on the year, shares of oil & gas companies have offered decent diversification amid a very busy January on the macro front.

The coming days will bring company-specific story updates, including an unusual Sunday-night earnings event. Next week’s XOM and CVX Q4 reports will be particularly revealing, given early-year geopolitical developments.

1 BKR, ORATS, January 21, 2026, ORATS Dashboard

Original Post

data.title: IGV Vs. IGPT: Performance Gap Shows AI Is Starting To Eat Software
data.description:
For years, two software ETFs moved almost in lockstep. That changed recently. These ETFs are the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) and the Invesco AI and Next Gen Software ETF (IGPT). Over the past year, IGPT has outperformed IGV by about 33 percentage points on a total-return basis, with most of the divergence occurring over the past six months. This may reflect the market's fear that software SaaS companies' moats are weakening, while AI picks-and-shovel stocks get favored as key beneficiaries of the data center boom.

One caveat is that IGPT shows a long price history, but in reality the ETF had a strategy change in August 2023. This involved renaming the ticker from PSJ to the current IGPT. The change also meant replacing the underlying reference benchmark with the "STOXX World AC NexGen Software Development Index". The strategy change for the ETF started in August 2023, making it significantly more "AI focused". This resulted in exposure to more chip and hardware companies that have benefited from the AI theme. Even after the change in strategy for IGPT, both ETFs had similar performances. As seen in the graph below, most of the performance difference has occurred in the last six months.

Software's Moat Under Attack

Software companies have historically earned strong competitive moats for a few reasons. Switching costs are a key one. Changing a core platform can be both technically and operationally risky.

Some businesses also benefit from network effects. Microsoft (MSFT) is a good example, as many organizations use Office and Windows because most employees are already familiar with them, and employees learn them because so many employers require them. In other cases, proprietary datasets, integrations, or patents add further strength to the competitive moats.

We believe the "AI impact" on moats will be different for each company, and we discussed some examples in a previous article of companies at risk of disruption. AI coding is lowering the barriers to entry, reducing the time and costs required to build complex software. This could result in companies building their own tools, or startups building competing software at significantly reduced prices compared to the incumbents. AI agents can also help migrate data from one system to another, reducing switching costs. New AI-native startups could also ship software more quickly, and be more aggressive on pricing.

Portfolio Characteristics

There are some important differences in the portfolio characteristics of both ETFs that help explain the recent performance divergence. Both have significant exposure to software companies, but IGPT's largest sector weight is semiconductors.

This can be seen in the top holdings. IGPT includes several chip and semiconductor equipment names that have been strong performers, while IGV's top positions skew more toward application software. The ETFs also differ in their "Mag 7" exposure. Both have meaningful weight in mega-cap tech, but IGPT carries more of it, whereas IGV's current top-ten list is dominated by software names, with Microsoft standing out as the main mega-cap in the top holdings. Adobe (ADBE) is the only top-ten overlap between the two, and IGPT's top holdings include tech-heavy communications sector exposure such as Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META).

Top 10 Holdings IGV Top 10 Holdings IGPT
Microsoft Micron Technology
Palantir Technologies Alphabet Inc
Oracle Advanced Micro Devices
Salesforce NVIDIA
AppLovin Meta Platforms
Intuit SK hynix
Palo Alto Networks Intuitive Surgical
Adobe Intel
CrowdStrike QUALCOMM
ServiceNow Adobe

Future Outlook

The market appears to have taken the disruption risk for software companies more seriously starting in the second half of last year. This year fears have intensified as AI coding tools have gotten better. NVIDIA's Huang even praised Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude at Davos, saying they use it "all over" at NVIDIA.

Earnings projections for software companies are projected to decelerate, while chip makers are expected to see accelerated growth. This has led to the performance divergence continuing between IGV and IGPT year-to-date. The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) has performed even better, with an over 15% total return this year.

It is our opinion that the disruption threat for some software companies is real, but they should be evaluated on an individual basis. Certain companies are more entrenched and difficult to displace than others. The market appears to be punishing companies in a relatively indiscriminate manner, with the likes of Intuit (INTU), Adobe, and Salesforce (CRM), all plunging after new coding tools were released this year.

Valuations

Despite the weak performance delivered by software stocks, they are still far from deep value territory. Shares in the IGV ETF currently trade at a Price/Earnings multiple of roughly 42x. This is higher than the close to 30x Price/Earnings multiple average for IGPT. If hardware and chip stocks see faster profit growth, this could result in a continued performance divergence in favor of IGPT. One advantage IGV has is a slightly lower expense ratio of 0.39%, compared to IGPT's 0.56%.

Risks

We do believe there is real AI disruption risk for many software stocks, but not for all. We would therefore avoid a software focused ETF like IGV, and rather look at individual companies and their real disruption risk. Smaller, less entrenched software companies are likely to be the ones most at risk, compared to giants like Microsoft. We see a valuation risk with both ETFs, as the average valuation appears difficult to justify in both cases.

Both ETFs have stocks that are trading based on hypergrowth narratives, which makes them highly susceptible to a sharp price readjustment. A good example is Oracle (ORCL), where sentiment toward the stock quickly changed from an AI beneficiary, to a high-risk stock overly exposed to AI data center infrastructure.

Takeaway

The key insight we got from comparing these two ETFs is that the market has started to take software AI disruption more seriously. The performance divergence that really began in the second half of 2025 has accelerated. Comments like those from NVIDIA's Huang at Davos add credibility that AI coding is getting more powerful. Still, we believe it is a mistake to treat all software companies equally, as their competitive moats are different, and some could even benefit from AI if they incorporate its use in a smart way. With chipmakers, we see a risk that valuations could be pricing too much growth. In the case of software, we believe the AI disruption risk is real for many companies.

data.title: FuboTV: Huge Scalability News, Need To Profits First
data.description:
FuboTV Inc. (FUBO) is a U.S.‑based live streaming television service that started in 2015 with a focus on live sports and has since expanded into a broader sports, news and entertainment platform. The company aims to replace traditional cable TV by aggregating premium channels, including major sports packages, via internet streaming across the United States, Canada and Spain. The company’s core business has centered on attracting paid subscribers to its live TV service.

They have historically struggled with profitability and slender subscriber growth in a fiercely competitive streaming landscape dominated by larger players like YouTube TV. The stock is down massively since its IPO a decade ago and has dropped another 45% since this past September.

FUBO dropped some big news that is coming to life here in early 2026 on an agreement that could be the engine the company needs to start generating profits. However, I am still leery about the past performance on this stock based on slow revenue, weak cash flow, and the competitive market in attempts to gain subscribers. I have FUBO as a hold but am very curious to see how synergies play out with the new finalized agreement.

Hulu + Live TV

Earlier this month, FUBO came to an agreement with Disney to combine with their Hulu + Live TV segment to form a combined virtual Multichannel Video Programming Distributor (MVPD). The discussion began earlier in 2025, and received shareholder approval this past fall, with finalizations now taking place in Q1 2026.

Under the agreement, Disney will own 70% of FUBO. Operationally, FUBO will continue to run as its own streaming service, but now the stock represents ownership in Fubo and Hulu + Live TV business as a 30% stake. Management notes that the addition allows for greater consumer choice and flexibility, along with enabling the firm to strengthen their balance sheet and take strides toward positive cash flow.

FUBO shareholders will benefit from the synergies of the combination, and a jump in revenue should be expected. It will be interesting to see how the numbers roll out for Q1 2026 and the remainder of the year, and what the management tone is on synergies, as there really aren’t any initial number projections yet as to how this will affect sales, profits, and bottom-line. Nonetheless, this is an exciting time for FUBO, which was desperately needed as the stock has been riding the “dog days” for quite some time. It will be interesting to watch how this plays out for the stock, but even with the agreement, it’s hard to ignore the lack of growth organically.

Sales & Subscribers

The subscriber base will gain substantially on the books moving forward with the merger of Hulu + Live TV. Internally, though, subscribers were up 1.1% YoY, with loss of subscribers outside of their North America region. A service built on scaling subscriptions must show consistent and accelerating subscriber additions to justify its valuation and lack or profit, but FUBO has not be able to grow. Demand is seasonal and volatile with live sports, which remains a key element to their business. They have larger rivals that are hard to compete with, but the Hulu + Live TV should certainly help here, as now their combined subscriber base is near 6 million in North America.

Sales were growing quickly for FUBO in its earlier years, but have tapered off completely since the end of 2023, on stagnant subscriber numbers. The stock gained through 2020 and 2021 as revenue was climbing quickly but has since stalled. Prior earnings reports have mentioned pricing power as a problem in finding the happy medium of remaining competitive to consumers while trying to generate profits, as margins are stuck in the red. Hulu + Live TV will help with leverage and scale, as now, together combined with the current FUBO base, they are the sixth largest Pay TV service in the United States.

At the end of the day, revenue will clearly mount gains quickly once these synergies begin. I will be curious to see how investors react when these numbers arise, as well as excited to see how FUBO bottom-line (hopefully) improves as the agreement moves along.

Cash Flow

Operating cash flow has been consistently negative for years, and it has been a clear struggle for the business. Low subscriber growth, content costs, and lack of scale have been drivers to the inefficiencies of their core business. This is a big reason I don’t have the stock as a buy. I do really like what can happen with the Hulu + Live TV situation, but the internal battle to make profit year after year is very evident.

Moving forward, cash flow should be propelled by the new deal in several ways. Live TV is heavier in fixed costs like content contracts and tech infrastructure. With the addition of millions of total subscribers, these costs get spread over a much larger base, meaning more cash per subscriber. Hulu + Live TV will also bring a more premium entertainment audience and higher ad fill rates, which are generally higher in margin. Lastly, improving the audience base outside of sports, will help create more consistent cash flow too.

It's hard to predict what the numbers will look like, and most recently, FUBO was $-166.4 million for the year in cash from operations. I think there’s a good chance by end of fiscal year 2026, FUBO could be in the green for operational cash, but before buying this stock, I still need to see how a few quarters of earnings and trends look moving forward.

Conclusion

The combination with Hulu + Live TV introduces a credible path to greater scale, improved bargaining power with content partners, and potential cost efficiencies that Fubo could not realistically achieve on its own. If executed well, the deal could meaningfully strengthen the company’s competitive position and improve long-term economics in an otherwise unforgiving live-TV streaming market. That strategic optionality gives the stock upside that should not be ignored.

At the same time, meaningful risks remain. Fubo’s internal organic growth in revenue and subscribers has been modest, and the company has yet to produce consistent positive operating cash flow, suggesting structural challenges are a continuing weakness.

If cash flow and profitability soar through the first few quarters of 2026, I will be reconsidering a buy for this stock. Hold right now and wait to see how a few quarters play out from the big agreement.

data.title: UVIX: Hold Despite Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Overview

The VIX futures term structure has been in contango for almost two months now, punishing UVIX, which is constantly rebalancing by selling close-to-expiry futures and buying more expensive longer-dated futures. Furthermore, the intraday VIX spike on January 15, driven by Fed and semiconductor export uncertainty, left UVIX down on the day, demonstrating the fragility of long-VIX products in generally low-volatility environments. In this article, I will explore the emergence of 0DTE options in the S&P 500 market as well as structural issues with UVIX, arguing that the product is not suitable in this environment.

The VIX tracks the 30-day implied volatility of the S&P 500, derived from the premiums of options with 23-37 days to expiry. Thus, any shorter-dated options are completely excluded from the index and thus don’t impact the VIX futures market. VIX spikes when traders buy protection either as a hedge or as speculation of higher volatility. This activity used to be concentrated on >30 days to expiry options; however, there has been a massive shift towards hedging short-term events using 0DTE. CBOE reports: “SPX zero day to expiry (0DTE) options trading has grown more than five-fold over the past 3 years, now averaging almost 2M contracts a day. What is driving that growth?... We estimate that retail now makes up around 50-60% of SPX 0DTE trading.” This was in part driven by the introduction of Tuesday/Thursday 0DTE expiry dates. Now, 0DTE makes up almost 57% of SPX options.

Thus, a massive portion of the market’s expectations is structurally detached from the VIX. Only longer-term increases in hedging activity or speculation can drive sustained VIX spikes. Since the liberation-day volatility of April 2025, the market has been relatively calm, particularly at the turn of the new year when VIX hovered between 13 and 15. Moreso, contango has persisted, typical of a calm market environment. As I mentioned in previous articles, VIX-linked ETFs are structurally disadvantaged amidst contango, as they are forced to sell cheap contracts that have suffered from time decay and buy more expensive contracts. In other words, they are holding futures while they decay. A large market of hedge funds and retail traders shorting volatility takes advantage of this dynamic, profiting in relatively calm market regimes with upward-sloping futures structures.

UVIX Implications

Any VIX-linked ETF struggles with this dynamic, but UVIX, a 2x leveraged ETF, naturally struggles even more than its less leveraged counterparts. Thus, there is a high bar to pass when arguing for a buy on UVIX. Specifically, there must be a probable catalyst, a favorable term structure, and reason to believe that the catalyst will impact longer-dated expiries as opposed to 0DTE markets. Anything short of this risks falling prey to the massive negative roll yield (the effect of buying expensive and selling cheap futures) endemic to UVIX. A telling example of this danger occurred in mid January. Currently, the term structure shows a February futures to spot spread of approximately 3, implying a 4-6% monthly decay, which is doubled by UVIX leverage.

On January 15, on the back of news of President Trump subpoenaing Jerome Powell and threatening Fed independence once again, in addition to the announcement of a 25% tariff on high-performance AI chips, the VIX spiked intra-day to 22.5. However, within hours, it collapsed back down to 15.84, and UVIX actually ended the day down. The short-term volatility was absorbed, and VIX mean-reverted extremely quickly.

Sustained volatility regimes invert the futures curve, creating backwardation, which benefits long-VIX ETFs (selling more expensive futures, buying cheaper futures). However, the futures structure has not experienced sustained backwardation since last April, a sign of a sustained low-volatility environment that has contributed to the massive decay of UVIX. This is closely connected to the January 15 event, as it demonstrates just how unique market conditions must be to make UVIX a smart hedge or speculation instrument.

Potential Upward Catalysts

Nevertheless, there are some compelling catalysts in the near future that contribute to an overall uncertain market condition. On January 21, the Supreme Court will hear a case that determines whether President Trump has the authority to fire Fed officials, marking a determining moment for US monetary policy independence. Secondly, on the same Wednesday, VIX futures and options expire, marking a rebalancing day for UVIX and other VIX-linked ETFs.

This creates a “pinning effect”, as market makers with large positions at certain VIX strikes are forced to constantly balance their hedges due to structurally higher gamma near expiries. This hedging leads to futures prices gravitating to wherever most expiries are. After the expiry date, this tethering ceases, and futures prices move more freely based on market events. Thus, there could be elevated activity post-expiry. Between January 27-28, the FOMC is also meeting to decide interest rates in one of its eight meetings per year. While there is a 84% chance that policy will be unchanged, changing forward guidance could induce volatility.

It could also be argued that the aforementioned low volatility in the last few months is artificially low due to an inflated AI trade and accommodative monetary policy despite above-target inflation. Generally, there is a case to be made to go long-VIX as a hedge to broad-based market exposure, or as a strategic directional bet on underpriced volatility.

While this represents a lower possibility, increased talk around US involvement in Iran and Greenland has created additional geopolitical uncertainty. While these represent longer-term, more unlikely events that are not as meaningful regarding a short-term instrument such as UVIX, they must be kept on the radar.

Structural Risks

As a general reminder, it is worth noting the risks inherent to a leveraged product such as UVIX. Particularly, the product experiences beta slippage over time, especially during volatile markets. The product is designed to be held for short periods of time, as a hedge or as a speculative instrument, evidenced by the dramatic value decay just YTD. With that being said, I view VIXY, a 1x VIX futures-linked ETF, as a more effective hedge due to its relatively lower beta slippage and lower negative roll yield. However, it is again worth noting that any long-VIX ETF experiences a level of decay amidst an upward-sloping VIX futures curve. The risky reality is closely linked to my earlier note regarding the high bar that must be reached in order to rate UVIX a "buy". Only when individual risk tolerance is high, roll yield dynamics are favorable, and macro headwinds indicate probable, underpriced volatility, is this bar met.

Due to its daily tracking and leverage, this is not meant to be a buy-and-hold product (more information about risks associated with this can be found here). Furthermore, even when it is appropriate to buy the product, the aforementioned structural risks mean that it should represent a small part of a portfolio.

Conclusion

For these reasons, I view UVIX as neither completely attractive nor unattractive at this time. The structural difficulties caused by higher 0DTE volume and persistent contango mean that the bar to enter this leveraged long-volatility product is extremely high. Yet, several meaningful near-term catalysts could create either short-term or long-term volatility. UVIX is highly sensitive to movement in longer-dated SPX options prices, and could easily spike on meaningful news linked to these events. Thus, I rate UVIX a hold.
